metadata.subject: Science
metadata.description: Physics 12 is the capstone senior physics course, extending students' understanding into relativity, static equilibrium, circular motion, fields (gravitational, electric, magnetic), and momentum conservation. Students develop sophisticated mathematical problem-solving skills, apply vector analysis to complex physical systems, and connect physics concepts to cutting-edge technologies and phenomena. The course prepares students for post-secondary physics and engineering.
metadata.general_description: An advanced senior physics course covering frames of reference and special relativity, equilibrium and circular motion, gravitational and electromagnetic fields, and momentum conservation — preparing students for post-secondary physics and engineering.

big_ideas.statement: Measurement of motion depends on our frame of reference.
big_ideas.elaboration: Relative motion demonstrates that velocity, displacement, and even time are not absolute — they depend on the observer's frame of reference. Einstein's special relativity extends this insight to show that the laws of physics are the same in all inertial frames, with profound consequences for space, time, and mass.

big_ideas.statement: Forces can cause linear and circular motion.
big_ideas.elaboration: Beyond linear dynamics, forces operating perpendicular to velocity cause circular motion — requiring centripetal force analysis. Static equilibrium (both translational and rotational) applies Newton's laws to stationary systems such as bridges, levers, and structures.

big_ideas.statement: Forces and energy interactions occur within fields.
big_ideas.elaboration: Gravitational, electric, and magnetic fields are the invisible structures through which forces act at a distance. Understanding field theory is essential for analysing planetary orbits, charged particle behaviour, and electromagnetic induction.

big_ideas.statement: Momentum is conserved within a closed and isolated system.
big_ideas.elaboration: The law of conservation of momentum — that the total momentum of an isolated system is constant — governs collisions, explosions, and rocket propulsion. Combined with impulse-momentum theorem, it provides a powerful framework for analysing interactions between objects.

competencies.raw: Demonstrate sustained intellectual curiosity; formulate multiple hypotheses about natural phenomena; predict quantitative outcomes.
i_can_statements: I can formulate complex questions about motion, forces, fields, and momentum in advanced physical systems.
I can formulate multiple hypotheses with specific quantitative predictions for physics investigations.
I can apply principles of relativity, circular motion, and field theory to predict physical outcomes.

competencies.raw: Collaboratively plan investigations; assess risks; collect reliable quantitative data using appropriate SI units and equipment.
i_can_statements: I can design advanced physics investigations with careful attention to variable control and precision.
I can collect quantitative data on forces, fields, and motion using appropriate laboratory instruments.
I can apply vector analysis and significant figures to complex physics data sets.

categories.name: Processing and Analyzing Data and Information
categories.raw: Processing and Analyzing Data and Information

competencies.raw: Seek patterns in data; construct and interpret graphs and models; draw evidence-based conclusions.
i_can_statements: I can identify patterns in physics data and use them to derive physical laws and constants.
I can construct and interpret position-time, velocity-time, and force diagrams for complex systems.
I can draw quantitative conclusions about fields, motion, and momentum from experimental data.

competencies.raw: Assess experimental methods; identify error sources; evaluate model validity; consider social and ethical implications.
i_can_statements: I can identify sources of systematic and random error in advanced physics experiments.
I can evaluate the validity and limitations of physical models including special relativity and field theory.
I can consider the social, ethical, and environmental implications of physics technologies.

competencies.raw: Design cooperative projects with real-world applications; implement multiple problem-solving strategies.
i_can_statements: I can apply physics principles to solve engineering design and real-world technology problems.
I can implement multiple problem-solving strategies including vector decomposition, field diagrams, and conservation laws.
I can connect physics innovation to technological development in areas such as medical imaging, GPS, and energy generation.

competencies.raw: Formulate theoretical models; present evidence-based arguments using scientific language and conventions.
i_can_statements: I can formulate and explain theoretical models for fields, relativistic effects, and momentum conservation.
I can communicate advanced physics concepts using mathematical notation, diagrams, and correct units.
I can write lab reports presenting sophisticated quantitative analysis and evidence-based conclusions.

areas.name: Frames of Reference and Special Relativity
areas.raw: Measurement of motion depends on our frame of reference.
topics: Relative velocity: frames of reference in 1D and 2D
Galilean relativity: classical addition of velocities
Einstein's postulates of special relativity: invariance of physical laws and constant speed of light
Time dilation: moving clocks run slow (t = γt₀)
Length contraction: moving objects appear shorter (L = L₀/γ)
Mass-energy equivalence: E = mc²
Relativistic momentum and energy: applications to particle physics

areas.name: Equilibrium and Circular Motion
areas.raw: Forces can cause linear and circular motion.
topics: Translational equilibrium: ΣF = 0 in two dimensions
Rotational equilibrium: Στ = 0; torque calculations
Centre of mass and centre of gravity
Uniform circular motion: centripetal acceleration and centripetal force (Fc = mv²/r)
Applications: banked curves, conical pendulum, circular orbital motion
Non-uniform circular motion: vertical circles and tension analysis

areas.name: Gravitational and Electromagnetic Fields
areas.raw: Forces and energy interactions occur within fields.
topics: Gravitational field: Newton's law of universal gravitation (F = Gm₁m₂/r²)
Gravitational field strength (g = GM/r²) and orbital mechanics
Kepler's laws: planetary motion and satellite orbits
Electric field: Coulomb's law (F = kq₁q₂/r²), field lines, and superposition
Electric potential and potential energy
Magnetic field: right-hand rule, force on moving charges (F = qvB), and current-carrying conductors
Electromagnetic induction: Faraday's law, Lenz's law, and applications
Transformers, generators, and AC/DC systems

areas.name: Momentum and Impulse
areas.raw: Momentum is conserved within a closed and isolated system.
topics: Linear momentum: p = mv
Impulse: J = FΔt = Δp
Conservation of momentum: closed and isolated systems
Elastic collisions: both momentum and kinetic energy conserved
Inelastic collisions: momentum conserved, kinetic energy not conserved
Perfectly inelastic collisions: objects stick together
Explosions: momentum conservation applied to fragmentation
Applications: airbags, rockets, ballistics, and particle collisions
